The surgeon performs a left hemicolectomy, making a small incision in the upper abdomen to retrieve the specimen. The colectomy is followed by a colocolic end-to-end anastomosis using a hands-in technique. The specimen is successfully removed with minimal blood loss.
The surgeon begins colonic mobilization, inspecting the colon for vascular pedicles and any adhesions to the abdominal wall.

The surgeon observes the splenic flexure, which is adherent to the parietal peritoneum. The existing ports from the hepatotomy are being used, and an additional port may be required to aid in the mobilization of the descending colon.
The surgeon inserts a 5mm trocar for battery section, preparing for further surgical intervention.

The assistant grasps the distal transverse colon and pulls it upward to expose the left colic vessels. The surgeon identifies the left colic vessels at their root, with a portion of the pancreas visible protruding from the mesentery of the colon.

The surgeon begins dissection by dividing the peritoneum above the level of the duodenojejunal (DJ) flexure, revealing the root of the left side of the mesocolon and a major vessel. The peritoneum and its attachments are carefully taken layer by layer to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon continues dissection in a classical V pattern, focusing on clearing lymph nodes at the root of the mesentery. Despite dealing with stage four disease, the aim is to remove as many lymph nodes as possible.

The surgeon navigates along the anterior border of the pancreas, identifying the mesentery of the colon above and the pancreas below, while adjusting the surgical approach to move skyward.

The surgeon begins mobilization towards the descending colon, with the first sigmoid branch visibly arising from the left colic artery. The DG flexure is also visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects the mass along with delicate vessels, ensuring the active surgical field remains clear of the duodenum and other vital structures during the close space dissection.

The surgeon mobilizes the distal part of the colon, separating the descending colon from surrounding adhesions in the correct anatomical plane to facilitate a healthy anastomosis.
